moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the king's pawn two squares forward, staking our claim to the central squares and freeing both our bishop on f1 and our queen for future development. This classical first move controls the important d5 and f5 squares, giving us excellent piece coordination possibilities as the game unfolds. The e4 advance is part of our plan to dominate the center quickly and launch a kingside attack, while also allowing for rapid castling to safety. This move has been the choice of world champions for centuries because it leads to sharp, tactical games where White can press for an early advantage.

Our opponent has just played the highly unusual Goldsmith Defense, pushing the h-pawn to h5 on the very first move - this is considered one of the most eccentric and objectively weak defenses in chess. Black completely ignores the center and normal development principles, instead launching an immediate but premature pawn storm on the kingside that actually weakens their own king safety. This move doesn't contest any central squares, doesn't develop pieces, and creates long-term structural weaknesses that we can exploit throughout the game. We should respond by continuing with our natural development plan, building a strong center with moves like d3, Nf3, and Bc4, knowing that Black's king will likely remain unsafe for most of the game.
